title: Chapter 10 — Publishing, PIDs & preparing for reuse
Chapter 10 — Publishing, PIDs & preparing for reuse
Chapter 10 — Publishing, PIDs & preparing for reuse
Chapter 10 — Publishing, PIDs & preparing for reuse
text:
Persistent identifiers (PIDs)
Persistent identifiers provide stable references to research outputs and people.
Common examples include:

DOI (Digital Object Identifier) is a persistent, unique identifier used to reliably identify and locate a research output (e.g. datasets, code releases, preprints, and publications) over time. A DOI looks like this: 10.1371/journal.pcbi.1005510 It acts as a stable reference to an object, even if the web location (URL) changes.
ORCID (Open Researcher and Contributor ID) is a persistent digital identifier that uniquely identifies a researcher, independent of name variants, institutional moves, or changes over time. In practice, an ORCID iD is a 16-digit identifier (e.g. 0000-0002-1825-0097) that links a person to their research contributions across systems.
ROR ID is a persistent identifier for a research organisation, for example:

https://ror.org/02jx3x895 , (that one identifies University College London).
PIDs allow outputs to be linked across systems and versions, supporting attribution, discovery, and long-term access.







Credit and assessment



PIDs and well-described outputs support credit, attribution, and reuse across systems.
They also reduce friction when reporting outputs for institutional processes and assessment.

title: Chapter 10 — Publishing, PIDs & preparing for reuse
Chapter 10 — Publishing, PIDs & preparing for reuse
section: Context is part of the output

title: Chapter 10 — Publishing, PIDs & preparing for reuse
text:
Licences and reuse
A licence states what others may do with an output once they have lawful access. It does not determine who can access an output in the first place.
Publishing for reuse usually involves two decisions:

Access route (open, safeguarded, controlled, mediated).
Reuse licence (what reuse is permitted once access is granted).


Common licence families (what they mean in practice)









Output type
Typical licence family
What it enables
Common pitfalls




Papers, figures, reports, READMEs, training materials
Creative Commons (CC)
Reuse of text/figures with defined conditions
Restrictive CC variants can block legitimate reuse (e.g., translations, teaching adaptations)


Metadata records (dataset descriptions)
CC0 (often used)
Maximises discoverability and machine reuse
Still must not disclose confidential/sensitive information


Code / software
Open-source software licences (e.g. MIT/Apache/GPL)
Reuse, modification, redistribution of code
Licence mismatch across dependencies; unclear copyright ownership in collaborative code


Data
Context-dependent (may be CC0/CC BY where lawful)
Reuse where sharing is lawful and ethical
Governance overrides licensing: you cannot “licence away” confidentiality, consent limits, or contractual restrictions









A proportionate default (often suitable for ECR projects)




Text and documentation: prefer CC BY unless you have a clear reason to restrict adaptations.
Code: choose an explicit software licence (often MIT or Apache 2.0).
Metadata: consider CC0 to maximise interoperability.
Sensitive data: publish metadata + access conditions even when data cannot be open (FAIR ≠ open).





Publisher agreements and rights
For journal articles, what you can share (and when) is often governed by the publisher agreement (copyright transfer or an exclusive licence). Record:

which version you can share (preprint / accepted manuscript / version of record);
where you can deposit it (institutional repository, subject repository);
any embargo period;
the licence applied to the open version (if applicable).

(See the Resources and Further Reading chapter for links, training and institutional guidance.)

title: Chapter 10 — Publishing, PIDs & preparing for reuse
text:
Responsible sharing and responsible reuse
Sharing outputs responsibly involves:

respecting ethical, legal, and contractual constraints;
choosing appropriate access routes;
applying clear licences.

Enabling responsible reuse additionally requires sufficient documentation and context so others understand how, when, and for what purposes reuse is appropriate.
For journal articles and associated outputs, responsible publishing also includes adherence to recognised reporting standards (e.g. STROBE, PRISMA)1, which specify the minimum information needed for interpretation and reuse.
Relevant standards are listed in the Resources and Further Reading.
Many journals now require explicit data and code availability statements. These clarify what is available, under what conditions, and why any restrictions apply.
Preparing outputs for reuse therefore involves a minimum package: clear documentation, structured metadata, an explicit licence, and a statement of access conditions.

title: Chapter 10 — Publishing, PIDs & preparing for reuse
section: Licensing is not the same as access
text:
Licensing is not the same as access
Licences define how an output may be reused once access is granted.
They do not determine who can access the output in the first place.
For example:

data may be openly accessible but licensed for limited reuse;
data may be access-controlled but licensed permissively once access is approved;
metadata may be openly available even when underlying data are restricted.

Separating decisions about access (open, controlled, mediated) from decisions about licensing supports clearer governance and more appropriate reuse.
Licences should be selected deliberately for each output type (text, data, code), and documented alongside access conditions.

title: Chapter 10 — Publishing, PIDs & preparing for reuse
text:
Sustainability and publishing
Well-prepared outputs reduce downstream burden.
Clear documentation, stable releases, and archived outputs:

reduce repeated clarification requests;
avoid unnecessary recomputation;
support long-term reuse without ongoing effort.

Publishing is therefore part of sustainable research practice. Choices about what to publish, how to document it, and where to archive it directly affect reproducibility and long-term sustainability.

title: Chapter 10 — Publishing, PIDs & preparing for reuse
Chapter 10 — Publishing, PIDs & preparing for reuse
text:
Exercises (5–10 minutes)

List the outputs from a recent project.
Which have persistent identifiers, and which do not?
Pick one output.
What context and licence would someone else need to reuse it responsibly?

title: Chapter 10 — Publishing, PIDs & preparing for reuse
text:
Key takeaways

Publishing is a process of releasing research outputs, not a single event.
Persistent identifiers support credit, linkage, and traceability.
Preprints enable early dissemination but require careful interpretation.
Licences are essential for enabling lawful reuse.
Publishing well supports reproducibility and sustainability.

title: Chapter 1 - Why Open?
text: Why Open?

title: Chapter 1 - Why Open?
Chapter 1 - Why Open?
text:
Why openness now?
Open research is no longer a niche movement or an optional add-on. Across scientific research, concerns about irreproducible findings, undocumented analytical decisions, and inaccessible research outputs have led funders, journals, and institutions to raise expectations around transparency and robustness1–3
In practice, this shift is less about ideology and more about reducing avoidable risk: rework, errors, lost context, and fragile results that cannot be checked, reused, or defended. This course is designed to support proportionate improvements that fit real research constraints.
Open and reproducible research supports meaningful involvement of patients, participants, and communities by making assumptions, decisions, and limitations visible and contestable.

title: Chapter 1 - Why Open?
section: Key terms (used consistently in this course)
text:
Key terms (used consistently in this course)
These terms overlap, but they are not identical:

Open research: making research outputs, methods, and decisions accessible to others, where lawful, ethical, and appropriate.
Transparency: clearly documenting what was done, why it was done, and how decisions were made (including decisions not visible in the final paper).
Reproducibility: enabling someone else (or you in six months) to understand and, where possible, recreate the analytical steps and results using the same data and methods.

A project can be reproducible without being fully open (e.g. sensitive data). Conversely, sharing outputs is not sufficient for reproducibility if key steps or provenance are missing.






Reproducible is not the only “-ible”



In The Turing Way terminology, results may be described as:

reproducible (same data, same analysis),
replicable (different data, same analysis),
robust (same data, different analysis),
generalisable (different data, different analysis).

These distinctions are useful when deciding what level of checking or reuse is realistic for a given project. For a short explainer and visual summary, see The Turing Way Definitions page4.

title: Chapter 1 - Why Open?
text:
How to read this course
This course separates principles, practices, and tools:

Principles are the underlying aims (e.g. transparency, accountability, reuse).
Practices are the behaviours that implement those aims (e.g. versioning, documentation, sharing decisions).
Tools are the platforms that support practices (e.g. repositories, version control systems, workflow tools).

Tools change quickly; principles and practices travel well across projects and roles. This is why the course emphasises practices, while the Resources and Further Reading chapter centralises tool- and UCL-specific signposting.






New to UCL (or a new project team)?



Use the Resources and Further Reading chapter as your “where to go next” page: UCL routes, contacts, and copy-ready templates are maintained there so links only need updating in one place.

title: Chapter 1 - Why Open?
section: Minimum expectations in practice (UCL and UK funders)
text:
Minimum expectations in practice (UCL and UK funders)
Open research is treated as a default position, applied across the research lifecycle and implemented proportionately within ethical, legal, and contractual constraints.
In most UCL research contexts, you should assume the following baseline expectations apply somewhere in your project:

Plan data management proportionately, and document decisions (a Data Management Plan may be required by funders and is generally expected for funded projects and projects handling research data).
Make outputs findable and preservable: ensure data, code, protocols, and other outputs (or at least metadata describing them) can be located, interpreted, and retained appropriately.
Enable open access to publications through the institutional route, in line with funder and assessment requirements.
Record constraints explicitly (e.g. sensitive data, governance restrictions, IP, contractual limits) and document how they affect sharing, access, and retention.

This course shows how to meet these expectations through robust everyday practice. UCL-facing routes, contacts, and templates are maintained in Resources and Further Reading.

title: Chapter 1 - Why Open?
section: Where irreproducibility comes from (in normal workflows)

title: Chapter 1 - Why Open?
text:
Proportionality and constraints
Open and reproducible practice is not a one-size-fits-all checklist. Constraints may include personal or sensitive data, contractual obligations, intellectual property considerations, and limited time or resource.
Good practice means making constraints explicit, documenting decisions, and using appropriate infrastructure. Proportionality applies to how you implement open practices, not to whether you document and justify decisions.

title: Chapter 1 - Why Open?
section: Open research in practice: what this implies for day-to-day work
text:
Open research in practice: what this implies for day-to-day work
Open research is not only about making outputs publicly available at the end of a project. Across the research lifecycle, work should be organised, documented, and described so that others — including your future self — can understand:

what was done and why;
how results were produced;
what assumptions and decisions shaped the work;
what could (and could not) be reused, and under what conditions.

Three foundations support this:

Documentation: READMEs, protocols, decision logs, and data dictionaries that capture context and rationale.
Organisation: consistent folder structures, naming conventions, and versioning that reduce ambiguity and error.
Metadata: structured descriptions that make outputs interpretable and discoverable (including for restricted-access data).

The next chapter introduces minimum expectations for describing outputs through the FAIR principles (see Chapter 2: FAIR basics).

title: Chapter 1 - Why Open?
section: AI and Open Science and Reproducibility
text:
AI and Open Science and Reproducibility
Automated and AI-assisted tools can improve efficiency and insight, but introduce new reproducibility challenges: opaque decision-making, stochastic outputs, undocumented prompts or parameters, and rapid model version changes. Open research practices remain essential to ensure transparency, accountability, and reuse in AI-assisted workflows.

title: Chapter 1 - Why Open?
section: What you will do in later modules

title: Chapter 1 - Why Open?
text:
Exercises (5–10 minutes)

Choose one current or recent project you are involved in.
Write down one thing that would make it hard for someone else to understand your results in six months’ time.
Where would you record this? (e.g. project README, methods note, decision log)
Identify one constraint (ethical, legal, contractual, or practical) that limits what you can share openly.
Where is that constraint recorded now — and where should it be recorded? (e.g. DMP, ethics documentation, governance notes, project README)

If you are unsure what “good enough” looks like for these artefacts, see the relevant templates in Resources and Further Reading.

title: Chapter 1 - Why Open?
text:
Key takeaways

Open research is now a baseline expectation, not an optional ideal.
Reproducibility problems usually arise from everyday workflow issues, not misconduct.
Proportionate openness reduces risk, inefficiency, and avoidable error.
You do not need fully open data to practise open research — but you do need clear documentation and defensible decisions.
Chapter 2: FAIR basics translates these ideas into concrete, data-level practice.

title: Chapter 2 - FAIR basics
text: FAIR basics

title: Chapter 2 - FAIR basics
Chapter 2 - FAIR basics
text:
What FAIR means (in practice)
FAIR stands for Findable, Accessible, Interoperable, and Reusable1.
The FAIR principles describe how research outputs should be structured and described so that they can be:

discovered,
accessed under clear conditions,
correctly interpreted,
and reused over time.

FAIR applies across the research lifecycle and to data, code, software, workflows, and documentation, not only datasets2.
FAIR is not: - a requirement to make data open; - a single repository or platform; - a fixed checklist.
Instead, FAIR provides a shared language for designing outputs that remain usable beyond the immediate project.

title: Chapter 2 - FAIR basics
section: FAIR is not the same as “open”
text:
FAIR is not the same as “open”
FAIR does not mean publicly open.
Research outputs may be: - FAIR and open; - FAIR but access-restricted (e.g. personal or sensitive data); - FAIR with mediated or controlled access.
A common framing is “as open as possible, as closed as necessary”. This distinction is especially important in health, social, and population research and is discussed further in Chapter 8 – Responsible & sustainable research.






FAIR still applies when data cannot be shared



When data cannot be shared openly, FAIR principles still apply to metadata, documentation, and access conditions describing the existence and nature of the output.

title: Chapter 2 - FAIR basics
text:
Findable: how outputs are discovered
Findable means that research outputs can be discovered by others, including people outside the original project.
In practice, findability is supported by mechanisms such as:

persistent identifiers (e.g. DOIs for datasets, ORCID iDs for researchers);
descriptive metadata that can be indexed by search systems;
stable locations (e.g. repositories rather than personal storage).

Findability is primarily about being locatable, not about being downloadable. An output can be findable even if access is restricted.

title: Chapter 2 - FAIR basics
section: Accessible: how access is defined and managed
text:
Accessible: how access is defined and managed
Accessible means that it is clear how an output can be accessed, under what conditions, and by whom.
Accessibility does not imply openness. Instead, it requires:

a clear access route (open, mediated, or restricted);
transparent access conditions;
persistence of metadata even if the data themselves are withdrawn or embargoed.

From a FAIR perspective, knowing why something cannot be accessed is as important as knowing how it can be accessed.

title: Chapter 2 - FAIR basics
section: Interoperable: how outputs can be interpreted correctly

title: Chapter 2 - FAIR basics
section: Reusable: how outputs can be used again

title: Chapter 2 - FAIR basics
section: FAIR applies to more than datasets

title: Chapter 2 - FAIR basics
section: FAIR, data management, and DMPs: how they relate
text:
FAIR, data management, and DMPs: how they relate
FAIR principles interact with, but are distinct from, other research practices:

FAIR provides design principles for structuring outputs.
Data management practice implements those principles through organisation, documentation, and versioning.
Data Management Plans (DMPs) describe how those practices will be applied, justified, and resourced.

This course treats FAIR as the conceptual foundation, with implementation addressed in later chapters and supported by institutional infrastructure.

title: Chapter 2 - FAIR basics
text:
Proportionality and constraints
FAIR is not all-or-nothing.
Projects differ in scale, sensitivity, duration, and resources. Good FAIR practice means making proportionate, defensible choices, documenting constraints, and being explicit about limitations.
This course focuses on FAIR considerations that are realistic for early-career researchers working within ethical, legal, and institutional boundaries.

title: Chapter 2 - FAIR basics
section: Where FAIR is implemented later in the course
text:
Where FAIR is implemented later in the course
In this course:

Minimum metadata standards are introduced in the Chapter 4 — Data Management Plans (UCL).
Planning and justification of FAIR actions are addressed in the Chapter 4 — Data Management Plans (UCL).
Templates, tools, and institutional routes are collected in Resources and Further Reading.

The next chapter focuses on project-level organisation and workflows, building directly on these principles.

title: Chapter 2 - FAIR basics
text:
Exercises (5–10 minutes)

Choose one research output you are working with (data, code, or documentation).
Which FAIR element (Findable, Accessible, Interoperable, or Reusable) do you think is currently weakest — and why?
Identify one constraint (ethical, legal, technical, or practical) that affects how FAIR could be applied to that output.
Where should that constraint be recorded later in the project?

title: Chapter 2 - FAIR basics
text:
Key takeaways

FAIR describes how research outputs should be structured and described, not whether they are open.
Findable, Accessible, Interoperable, and Reusable each address different aspects of long-term usability.
FAIR applies to data, code, documentation, and workflows.
FAIR principles inform — but do not replace — data management practice or DMPs.
Later chapters translate FAIR principles into concrete project-level actions.

title: Chapter 3 - Project organisation and continuity
text: Project organisation for reproducible research

title: Chapter 3 - Project organisation and continuity
Chapter 3 - Project organisation and continuity
Chapter 3 - Project organisation and continuity
Chapter 3 - Project organisation and continuity
Chapter 3 - Project organisation and continuity
text:
Versioning: principles, not tools
Versioning is essential for understanding how a project evolves over time. Crucially, versioning principles apply to all research materials, not only to code2.
It is useful to distinguish between two related but distinct practices.

File versioning (data, documents, outputs)
File versioning applies to data files, documents, and other non-code outputs. At a minimum, good practice involves:

retaining historical versions rather than overwriting files;
using clear, consistent version identifiers;
recording what changed and why.

This can be achieved using structured file naming and simple change logs, without specialised tools. Minimum examples and a changelog template are provided in the Resources and Further Reading chapter.


Code version control (e.g. Git)
Code version control systems are designed for tracking changes to scripts and software. They support:

fine-grained tracking of changes;
collaboration across multiple contributors;
reversible experimentation.

Not all projects require formal code version control, but where code is central to analysis, its use substantially improves transparency and reproducibility.
This course emphasises principles before tools. Tool-specific guidance and examples are signposted in the Resources and Further Reading chapter.

title: Chapter 3 - Project organisation and continuity
text:
Roles, responsibilities, and continuity
Reproducible projects make roles explicit.
Good practice includes:

documenting who is responsible for data, code, and documentation;
agreeing communication norms and review points;
onboarding new contributors with a short guide to project conventions.

Offboarding is equally important. When people leave a project, their context, assumptions, and decisions should not leave with them.

title: Chapter 3 - Project organisation and continuity
text:
Project organisation across the lifecycle
Organisation is not a one-off task.
Structures, documentation, and metadata should evolve as a project moves from setup to analysis to dissemination. Use milestones (e.g. protocol finalised, data locked, analysis frozen) as prompts to review and update organisation and records.
This helps ensure that published outputs reflect real working practices rather than retrospective reconstruction.
These organisational practices underpin later chapters on documentation (Chapter 6), code quality (Chapter 7), and automation (Chapter 9).

title: Chapter 3 - Project organisation and continuity
Chapter 3 - Project organisation and continuity
text:
Exercises (5–15 minutes)

Look at the top level of a current project.
Is there a project README describing purpose, outputs, and conventions?
Identify one recent decision in that project.
Is it recorded with context and rationale? Add it to a log if not.

title: Chapter 3 - Project organisation and continuity
text:
Key takeaways

Project organisation is foundational for reproducibility and collaboration.
Project-level metadata improves discoverability and continuity.
Versioning principles apply to data, documentation, and code.
Explicit roles and decision records reduce risk and knowledge loss.
Templates and examples are centralised in the Resources and Further Reading chapter.

title: Chapter 4 — Data Management Plans (UCL)
text: Data Management Plans (DMPs)

title: Chapter 4 — Data Management Plans (UCL)
Chapter 4 — Data Management Plans (UCL)
section: What a DMP is (and is not)
text:
What a DMP is (and is not)
A Data Management Plan describes how data will be1–3:

created or collected;
stored, documented, and protected;
retained, shared, or archived.

This includes describing what data and materials will be collected or reused, how they are generated, and which data types are in scope. A DMP is not a guarantee that plans will not change. Instead, it provides a transparent record of current intentions and decisions. Funders expect data management, storage, curation, and sharing to be explicitly resourced and costed, not treated as overhead.
Different data types (e.g. sensitive identifiers, geospatial data, qualitative materials, imaging, code/software outputs) require different levels of governance, documentation, and retention detail. The DMP should reflect this proportionately, without over-specifying implementation.
Where AI or machine-learning tools are used for data processing or analysis, the DMP should record what tools are used, how outputs are generated, and what can be documented or shared (e.g. model version, prompts, training data constraints), without over-committing to full reproducibility where this is infeasible.
Where patient or public contributors are involved, DMPs should reflect any implications for data access, consent, governance, and communication of outputs, even where contributors do not access raw data.

title: Chapter 4 — Data Management Plans (UCL)
Chapter 4 — Data Management Plans (UCL)
text:
Data description vs data governance
It is useful to separate two related but distinct elements:

Data description: what the data are, how they are structured, formats used, and how they are documented.
Data governance: who can access the data, under what conditions, how consent and approvals apply, and how storage, security, and backup responsibilities are defined, even if technical implementation is handled elsewhere.

Keeping these elements distinct improves clarity and supports both FAIR practice and ethical compliance. DMPs are also expected to describe how metadata will be created, maintained, and reviewed over time, without requiring field-level standards to be specified at this stage.

title: Chapter 4 — Data Management Plans (UCL)
section: What assessors look for in a good DMP

title: Chapter 4 — Data Management Plans (UCL)
section: Mapping good project practice to DMP sections
text:
Mapping good project practice to DMP sections
Many elements of a strong DMP directly reflect good internal project organisation. The table below illustrates this alignment.
Use this mapping to check that the DMP reflects plausible working practice. It reassures assessors that statements correspond to real practices, but you do not need to duplicate implementation detail in the DMP.

Project folder structure
→ DMP sections should state how data organisation and workflow separation will be handled (e.g., raw vs derived; storage locations).
README files and codebooks
→ DMP sections should state what documentation artefacts will exist and who maintains them.
Structured metadata
→ DMP sections should state how metadata will be captured, updated, and kept available (including when data access is restricted).
Versioning and change logs
→ DMP sections should state how changes to data, code, and outputs will be tracked (tool-agnostic).

Making this alignment explicit reassures assessors that the DMP reflects real working practices rather than aspirational statements.

title: Chapter 4 — Data Management Plans (UCL)
section: Planning for derived and intermediate data
text:
Planning for derived and intermediate data
DMPs should consider not only final datasets, but also:

derived datasets created during analysis;
intermediate files produced by scripts or workflows;
which data will be retained, archived, or discarded.

These decisions directly inform the archiving, preservation, and curation plans described in the DMP, including what will be retained beyond the active life of the project.
Chapter 5 covers how to structure and document data states and provenance in practice. In the DMP, focus on recording what you intend to retain, under what conditions, and why.

title: Chapter 4 — Data Management Plans (UCL)
text:
Sustainability and proportionality
Planning data management also has environmental implications.
DMPs should justify proportionate storage, retention, and compute plans in a way that supports reproducibility without unnecessary resource use. Practical options and examples are signposted in the Resources and Further Reading chapter.

title: Chapter 4 — Data Management Plans (UCL)
Chapter 4 — Data Management Plans (UCL)
section: Practical prompts when drafting a DMP

title: Chapter 4 — Data Management Plans (UCL)
text:
Exercises (5–10 minutes)

Review the DMP (or draft one) for a current project.
Is it clear which decisions are fixed and which may change?
Identify one type of derived or intermediate data.
Decide whether it should be retained, documented, or discarded, and where that decision should be recorded in the DMP.

title: Chapter 4 — Data Management Plans (UCL)
text:
Key takeaways

A DMP is a living record of decisions, not a static form.
Separating data description from governance improves clarity.
Planning should include derived and intermediate data.
Proportionate DMPs support reproducibility, ethics, and sustainability.

title: Chapter 5 - Organising and tidying data
text: Organising and tidying data

title: Chapter 5 - Organising and tidying data
Chapter 5 - Organising and tidying data
Chapter 5 - Organising and tidying data
text:
Raw, cleaned, and derived data
A useful organising principle is to separate data by state:

Raw data: as collected or received; never overwritten.
Cleaned data: errors corrected, formats standardised.
Derived data: variables or datasets created during analysis.

Keeping these states distinct preserves provenance and makes it possible to reproduce analyses from source data when needed.
This separation should be reflected both in folder structure and in documentation. Canonical structures are provided in the Resources and Further Reading chapter.

title: Chapter 5 - Organising and tidying data
text:
Data provenance and lineage
Data provenance describes where data come from, how they change, and how outputs are produced.
Recording provenance typically involves:

documenting transformation steps and assumptions;
linking derived outputs to scripts or workflows;
recording versions and reasons for change.

Explicit provenance supports audit, peer review, reuse, and defensibility. It is particularly important in regulated, sensitive, or administrative data contexts, where data access may be restricted but analytical transparency is still expected.
Key transformations (recoding, aggregation, linkage) should be summarised in documentation even when implemented in code.

title: Chapter 5 - Organising and tidying data
text:
Tidy data principles (practical view)
Tidy data conventions aim to make datasets easier to analyse and interpret1,2. In practice, this often means:

one variable per column;
one observation per row;
consistent naming and coding conventions.

Not all data can be perfectly tidy, and perfection is not the goal. Moving towards these principles improves legibility and reduces errors, even when constraints apply.
Further guidance and examples are signposted in the Resources and Further Reading chapter.

title: Chapter 5 - Organising and tidying data
text:
Missing data and exclusions
Handling missing data is not only technical but interpretive.
Good practice includes:

using explicit missing value codes;
documenting reasons for missingness where known;
recording exclusions and filtering decisions.

This information is often as important as the observed data itself and should be captured in documentation rather than inferred from code alone. Minimum documentation templates are provided in the Resources and Further Reading chapter.

title: Chapter 5 - Organising and tidying data
text:
File formats, naming, and versioning
Choices about formats, file names, and versions affect both longevity and reproducibility.
At a principle level, organised data should:

use stable, non-proprietary formats where possible;
avoid ambiguous or overloaded file names;
make changes traceable rather than overwriting files.

Rather than memorising rules, the key question is whether someone else could understand what changed, when, why, and by whom. Canonical examples and a changelog template are provided in the Resources and Further Reading chapter.

title: Chapter 5 - Organising and tidying data
text:
Minimum standards vs advanced practice
This course defines minimum standards that are achievable for most student and early-career research projects.
More advanced practices—such as automated validation, controlled vocabularies, or formal SOPs—are appropriate for large teams, regulated environments, or long-running studies. These are signposted in the Resources and Further Reading chapter and are not required for all projects.
The emphasis is on proportionality rather than completeness.

title: Chapter 5 - Organising and tidying data
Chapter 5 - Organising and tidying data
text:
Exercises (5–15 minutes)

Identify one dataset in a current project.
Can you clearly distinguish raw, cleaned, and derived versions?
Review how missing data are handled.
Are reasons for missingness or exclusion documented anywhere?

title: Chapter 5 - Organising and tidying data
text:
Key takeaways

Clear data organisation underpins reproducibility and interpretation.
Separating raw, cleaned, and derived data preserves provenance.
Provenance and documentation matter even when data are restricted.
Minimum standards should be achievable and proportionate.
All templates and examples are centralised in the Resources and Further Reading chapter.

title: Chapter 6 — Documentation, ELNs & SOPs
Chapter 6 — Documentation, ELNs & SOPs
text:
Why documentation matters
Documentation is the backbone of reproducible research. Without it, data and code lose meaning, decisions become opaque, and results are difficult to interpret or defend. This chapter focuses on documentation that directly supports reproducibility, continuity, and reuse, rather than all possible records generated during a project.
Most documentation failures are not due to lack of effort, but to unclear purpose: documenting everything everywhere, or documenting the wrong things. Over-documentation can be as harmful as under-documentation, creating noise, duplication, and maintenance burden without improving understanding.

title: Chapter 6 — Documentation, ELNs & SOPs
Chapter 6 — Documentation, ELNs & SOPs
section: Types of documentation (and what they are for)
text:
Types of documentation (and what they are for)
Different documentation artefacts serve different audiences and purposes:

READMEs: high-level orientation to the project (purpose, structure, how to run or navigate it).
Electronic Lab Notebooks (ELNs): chronological records of work, observations, and decisions.
Standard Operating Procedures (SOPs): stable instructions for repeatable tasks.
Code comments and docstrings: technical detail for specific scripts or functions.
Codebooks / data dictionaries: variable-level descriptions supporting interpretation and reuse.

Not all documents produced during a project are research documentation. Project plans, budgets, and timelines support delivery, while READMEs, ELNs, codebooks, and SOPs support reproducibility and reuse. Using the right format for the right purpose improves clarity and reduces duplication.

Documentation types and when to use each
Research projects typically require multiple forms of documentation, each serving a different purpose. Confusion often arises when a single document is used to do everything.
The table below summarises common documentation types and their primary role.









Documentation type
Primary purpose
Typical audience
When to use




README
Project or dataset orientation
New collaborators, reviewers, future you
At project or dataset level


Codebook / data dictionary
Variable-level description
Analysts, secondary users
For each dataset


ELN
Chronological record of work and decisions
Project team
During active research


SOP

SOPs and repeatability
SOPs support consistency across people and time.
Good SOPs are:

task-specific and scoped;
written for someone unfamiliar with the project;
versioned and reviewed periodically.

SOPs reduce variability, support training, and make quality control explicit.

title: Chapter 7 — Code quality & testing
text: Code quality, testing, and sustainable computation

title: Chapter 7 — Code quality & testing
Chapter 7 — Code quality & testing
section: Why code quality matters in research

title: Chapter 7 — Code quality & testing
text:
Testing as a research safeguard
Testing checks that code behaves as expected when inputs or assumptions change.
In practice, testing supports reproducibility by:

detecting errors early;
reducing reliance on manual checks;
supporting refactoring without breaking results.

Even simple tests (e.g. checking dimensions, ranges, or invariants) provide substantial benefits.






Sustainable coding: quality that lasts (and wastes less)



Sustainable code is not about perfection or performance for its own sake. It is about reducing unnecessary recomputation, storage, and rework while keeping analyses understandable, defensible, and maintainable.
Well-designed code saves time, energy, and attention — for you and for others.



What to look for when coding sustainably
The practices below focus on high-value, low-burden choices that improve both code quality and environmental sustainability.

Restartable, modular workflows
Structure scripts so analyses can resume from intermediate outputs rather than rerunning end-to-end. This reduces accidental full recomputation after small changes.
(Links to workflow tooling in Chapter 9.)
Incremental recomputation and caching
Save expensive intermediate results when justified, and recompute only what has changed. This is often the single biggest energy-saving decision in analysis code.
Profiling before optimisation
Use simple timing or memory checks to identify where time and compute are actually spent. Optimising unmeasured code often wastes more energy than it saves.
Algorithmic proportionality
Prefer methods and resolutions that are sufficient for the research question, rather than maximal by default (e.g. spatial resolution, iterations, precision). Small reductions here can dominate total compute cost.
Clear inputs, outputs, and assumptions
Make it obvious what each script expects, produces, and assumes. Ambiguity leads to reruns, duplication, and error-driven recomputation.
Lightweight, targeted testing
A small number of high-value checks (e.g. input validation, key invariants) can prevent long, expensive runs from failing late.
Readable structure over cleverness
Code that is easy to read, review, and modify is less likely to be rerun unnecessarily or replaced wholesale when changes are needed.



Why this matters beyond code style
Poorly structured or opaque code increases the likelihood of:

full reruns due to uncertainty or small errors;
duplicated data and intermediate files;
wasted compute from over-resolution or over-iteration;
abandonment and reimplementation of analyses.

In practice, inefficient code can make research effectively irreproducible. If analyses are too slow, expensive, or resource-intensive to rerun, this limits verification, sensitivity analysis, and extension by others. These constraints disproportionately affect researchers and teams with limited access to compute resources, creating hidden barriers to transparency and reuse. These costs are environmental, human, and organisational. Good-enough code quality is therefore a sustainability intervention, not just a technical preference.
(Responsibility and proportionality are discussed further in Chapter 8; workflow automation and dependency management are covered in Chapter 9.)


Documenting your code for clarity and reuse
Good documentation is not an afterthought — it’s part of writing code that others (and you later) can reliably understand and reuse.

Explain why: Comment the reasoning behind decisions, not just what the code does.

Write docs as you code: Update documentation alongside code to avoid outdated notes.

Use clear naming and structure: Consistent variable/function names and well-structured README/help files improve readability.

Document at multiple levels: Inline comments, function headers, and high-level project docs all have a role.

Include examples: Minimal usage examples help others understand expected inputs/outputs.

Keep docs up to date: Outdated documentation misleads and can cost more time than none at all.

Choose appropriate tools: Use roxygen2 (R), Sphinx (Python), or Markdown with docstrings to integrate docs with code.

Why it matters: well-documented code reduces confusion, unnecessary reruns, and onboarding friction — essential components of sustainable and reproducible research workflows.
AI-generated code should be treated like any other external contribution: reviewed, tested, and documented before use.

title: Chapter 7 — Code quality & testing
text:
Versioning, refactoring, and tests
Refactoring code is safer when tests are in place. The versioning principles introduced for data and documentation in (Chapter 5 - Organising and tidying data) apply equally to code. Scripts and analysis pipelines should not be overwritten without trace, and changes should be attributable, versioned, and recoverable. Code version control systems support this process, but the underlying expectation is consistency and traceability rather than tool choice.
Testing supports sustainable workflows by:

enabling incremental improvements;
preventing costly re-runs after small changes;
reducing fear of breaking working code.

This is particularly important in long-running or resource-intensive analyses.

title: Chapter 7 — Code quality & testing
text:
Key takeaways

Code quality underpins reproducibility and trust in results.
Testing reduces errors, rework, and unnecessary computation.
Inefficient code can make research effectively irreproducible.
Green coding focuses on reducing waste through clarity and efficiency.
Proportionate practices deliver most of the benefit.

title: Chapter 8 — Responsible research: roles, decisions, and accountability
Chapter 8 — Responsible research: roles, decisions, and accountability
text:
What responsibility means in practice
Responsible research is not limited to formal ethics approval or compliance checkpoints.
It involves anticipating consequences, making proportionate choices, and recording decisions throughout the project lifecycle.
Most responsibility challenges arise not from exceptional cases, but from routine decisions that are undocumented, implicit, or assumed to be someone else’s responsibility.

title: Chapter 8 — Responsible research: roles, decisions, and accountability
section: Responsibility as anticipation and decision capture
text:
Responsibility as anticipation and decision capture
The Turing Way frames responsibility as anticipatory.
Key questions include:

Who might be affected by this research?
What risks, burdens, or exclusions could arise?
What constraints limit what can be done, shared, or reused?

Responsible practice requires that these considerations are made explicit and recorded, alongside the rationale for decisions taken.
Documenting constraints and trade-offs allows decisions to be understood, revisited, and defended as contexts change.

title: Chapter 8 — Responsible research: roles, decisions, and accountability
Chapter 8 — Responsible research: roles, decisions, and accountability
section: Equity, inclusion, and accessibility as responsibility
text:
Equity, inclusion, and accessibility as responsibility
Equity and accessibility are not optional add-ons. They are operational aspects of responsible practice.
Examples include:

tools or workflows that require specialist infrastructure;
documentation that assumes prior technical or institutional knowledge;
processes that place hidden burdens on junior staff or collaborators.

Making expectations explicit, documenting decisions, and choosing proportionate approaches improves inclusion and collaboration.

title: Chapter 8 — Responsible research: roles, decisions, and accountability
section: Responsibility in sharing, restriction, and reuse
text:
Responsibility in sharing, restriction, and reuse
Responsible research extends to how outputs are shared and reused.
This includes:

setting clear access conditions and licences;
avoiding over-claiming reuse where constraints apply;
providing sufficient context to prevent misinterpretation.

These considerations link directly to Chapter 10 — Publishing, PIDs & preparing for reuse.
Responsible sharing does not mean sharing everything. It means being explicit about what can be shared, what cannot, and why.

title: Chapter 8 — Responsible research: roles, decisions, and accountability
Chapter 8 — Responsible research: roles, decisions, and accountability
Chapter 8 — Responsible research: roles, decisions, and accountability
text:
Exercises (5–10 minutes)

Identify one decision in a current project.
Who was responsible, and is the rationale documented?
Identify one constraint (ethical, legal, practical).
Is it recorded clearly enough for someone new to the project to understand?

title: Chapter 8 — Responsible research: roles, decisions, and accountability
text:
Key takeaways

Responsibility is an ongoing, documented practice.
Clear roles and accountability reduce risk and confusion.
Constraints and trade-offs should be recorded, not hidden.
Responsible sharing requires transparency about limits.
Institutional support is part of responsible research.

title: Chapter 9 — Automation & simple workflows
Chapter 9 — Automation & simple workflows
text:
What automation means in research
In research, automation does not mean complex pipelines or advanced tooling by default.
Automation means making the steps that turn inputs (data, parameters, code) into outputs (tables, figures, models, results) explicit, repeatable, and runnable without manual intervention.At its core, automation is a way of managing dependencies between inputs, transformations, and outputs.
A workflow can be automated even if it consists of only a small number of scripts, as long as:

the execution order is clear;
inputs and outputs are explicit;
results can be regenerated reliably.

Automation sits above individual scripts.
Code quality focuses on how code is written (Chapter 7); automation focuses on how pieces of work fit together.

title: Chapter 9 — Automation & simple workflows
text:
Why automate research workflows?
Manual workflows are fragile.
They rely on undocumented assumptions, memory, and manual steps that are difficult to reconstruct later — especially by collaborators, reviewers, or your future self.
Automation helps ensure that:

steps are run in the correct order;
inputs and outputs are clearly linked;
analyses can be re-run consistently.

This reduces error, supports review, and increases confidence that results are defensible and reproducible.By avoiding unnecessary full reruns, automation reduces compute waste and supports environmentally sustainable research practice.
For open research, automation is often the difference between sharing code and enabling reuse.

title: Chapter 9 — Automation & simple workflows
Chapter 9 — Automation & simple workflows
section: What to automate (and what not to)
text:
What to automate (and what not to)
Automation is most valuable when steps are routine, repeatable, and diagnostic.
In health and population research, many activities labelled as “exploratory” are in fact systematic checks that recur across datasets and projects. These are prime candidates for automation.

Strong candidates for automation
Automate steps that are:

Repeated across projects or data extracts, such as:

missingness summaries and patterns;
variable ranges and implausible values;
consistency checks (e.g. dates, identifiers, categories);
duplicate detection and basic record counts.

Required every time data are refreshed or updated, including:

re-running descriptive summaries;
regenerating standard tables or plots;
validating joins or linkage outputs.

Costly to rerun manually if missed, where late discovery leads to downstream rework.

Automating these steps improves comparability, creates an audit trail, and reduces the risk that important checks are skipped.


What should remain flexible
Not all exploratory work should be fully automated.
Activities that often benefit from flexibility include:

hypothesis generation and ad-hoc visualisation;
one-off transformations explored to understand unusual patterns;
conceptual restructuring of variables or outcomes.

However, even here, partial automation is often beneficial: saving scripts, parameterising functions, or re-running the same exploratory logic on updated data.
The goal is not to constrain thinking, but to avoid repeating the same diagnostic work from scratch.

title: Chapter 9 — Automation & simple workflows
text:
Automation and sustainability
Automation supports sustainability by reducing waste.
Well-designed workflows:

avoid repeated full pipeline runs;
reuse unchanged intermediate results;
prevent accidental recomputation.

From a sustainability perspective, automation operationalises the green coding principles introduced in Chapter 7 — Code quality & testing.
Reducing unnecessary computation saves time, energy, and storage — and improves reproducibility at the same time.

title: Chapter 9 — Automation & simple workflows
text:
Levels of automation (proportionate choices)
Automation can be lightweight.
Common forms include:

scripts that run analyses end-to-end;
simple rule-based pipelines (e.g. make-style logic);
scheduled or triggered runs for key steps.

The appropriate level depends on project scale, team size, and computational cost. More complex tooling is optional, not mandatory.
Starting small and scaling only when needed is usually the most sustainable choice.

title: Chapter 9 — Automation & simple workflows
Chapter 9 — Automation & simple workflows
Chapter 9 — Automation & simple workflows
text:

Could it be automated with a single script?
Pick one analysis output.
Can you trace which inputs and scripts produced it?

title: Chapter 9 — Automation & simple workflows
text:
Key takeaways

Automation supports reproducibility, transparency, and efficiency.
Managing dependencies is the core benefit of automation.
Not all steps need to be automated.
Automation reduces compute waste and supports sustainability.
Start simple and scale only when needed.

text: Wrap-up and next steps

title: Chapter 11 — Wrap-up & next steps
Chapter 11 — Wrap-up & next steps
section: What this course set out to do
text:
What this course set out to do
This course has focused on practical, proportionate practices that support open and reproducible research in real institutional contexts.
It has aimed to:

reduce avoidable errors and inefficiencies;
improve clarity, continuity, and defensibility;
support collaboration and responsible reuse.

It is not a comprehensive technical manual, nor a checklist to be completed.






Baseline expectations (UCL ECR “minimum viable reproducibility”)



If you do only these things, you will meet a sensible baseline for transparent, defensible research practice (including when data cannot be open):

Project README at the root (purpose, structure, conventions, contacts).
Decisions recorded (decision log or ELN entries for key changes/assumptions).
Raw vs derived separated (never overwrite raw; document transformations).
Re-runnable analysis (scripted steps; basic checks/tests where feasible).
Planned sharing and access (DMP/governance notes + clear access route).
Publish with identifiers and clarity (ORCID, DOIs where appropriate; licence + access conditions; availability statements).

Use Resources and Further Reading for copy-ready templates and UCL routes.

title: Chapter 11 — Wrap-up & next steps
Chapter 11 — Wrap-up & next steps
text:
Practices, not tools
Throughout the course, the emphasis has been on practices rather than specific tools.
Tools will change. Principles such as clarity, documentation, versioning, and transparency endure.
Choosing tools that support these practices in your context is more important than adopting any particular platform.

Open and reproducible research is incremental and contextual.
“Good enough” practice depends on risk, scale, and constraints.
Practices matter more than specific tools.

Onboarding: first actions on a new project
Use this when you join a project (or onboard someone else).

Find or create the project README (purpose, scope, structure, contacts).
Confirm data access and governance (what you can/can’t do; where it’s documented).

Identify the analysis entry point (main script / workflow) and the environment notes.
Find the decision log / ELN and record your first assumptions and uncertainties.
Check expected outputs (papers, datasets, code releases) and likely release points.

title: Resources and Further Reading
text:
Essential metadata (minimum standard)
Supports Chapter 5 — Organising and tidying data
Referenced by Chapters 6 (documentation) and 10 (publishing and reuse).

Essential Metadata Checklist (minimum standard)
When documenting a dataset, code release, protocol, or other output, include at least:

Title
Creators / contributors (including ORCID where possible)
Dates (created; last updated)
Description (what it is; why it exists; what it can and cannot support)
Version
Methods / provenance summary (how it was generated; key transformations)
Geography / time coverage (if relevant)
File formats and any required software
Access conditions (open / controlled / mediated; who to contact)
Licence (or explicit statement if licensing is constrained)
Funding / grant (if relevant)
Persistent identifier (DOI where appropriate; or stable URL + repository record)







Minimum rule of thumb



If a competent colleague can answer what it is, where it came from, how to access it, how to cite it, and what they are allowed to do with it, your metadata is likely “good enough” for most purposes.




Expanded metadata templates (Advanced / reference)

General research metadata template (provenance, methods, versions, licences, access conditions, related outputs)

Spatial/geocoded metadata template (CRS, extent, resolution, temporal coverage, processing)

(Keep these as copy-ready templates or separate downloadable files, then link them here.)

title: Resources and Further Reading
text:
Project organisation templates (copy-ready)
These templates define project-level coordination conventions.
Data-state conventions (raw, derived, documentation) are interpreted in Chapter 5 and applied here.

Baseline folder structure
project-root/
├── README.md
├── data/
│   ├── raw/
│   ├── derived/
│   └── documentation/
├── analysis/
│   ├── scripts/
│   └── outputs/
├── docs/
├── logs/
└── outputs/


File naming and versioning (minimum standard)
Recommended elements

short descriptive name
date (ISO): YYYYMMDD
version identifier (e.g. v01)
no spaces or special characters

Examples
survey_responses_raw_20240315_v01.csv
survey_responses_clean_20240318_v02.csv
analysis_model1_20240402_v03.R
protocol_amendment_20240510_v01.docx


Versioning cheat-sheet
Create a new version when:

cleaning/transformation changes;
analysis logic/parameters change;
outputs are regenerated using modified inputs;
errors are corrected.

Record:

version identifier;
date;
what changed (1–2 lines);
who changed it.



Optional: code version control (Git) (Advanced / reference)
Appropriate when code is central and/or multiple contributors edit scripts.
Key principles:

commit little and often with meaningful messages;
avoid committing large data files;
use tags/releases for milestones.

ELNs

Electronic lab notebooks (ELNs): good practice (The Turing Way) https://book.the-turing-way.org/reproducible-research/rdm/rdm-elns/

SOPs and essential research documents (health contexts)
Particularly relevant for clinical, NHS-linked, or regulated research.

These clarify what must be documented in regulated environments and how SOPs fit alongside READMEs and ELNs.






Documentation rule of thumb



If a colleague outside your project can understand what was done, why it was done, and under what constraints, your documentation is likely sufficient.

title: Resources and Further Reading
section: Code quality, testing, and sustainable software
text:
Code quality, testing, and sustainable software
Supports Chapter 7
These resources support writing clear, testable, and maintainable research code that can be understood, rerun, and reused over time. They focus on principles and proportionate practices rather than specialist tooling.

Core guidance on code quality and testing

The Turing Way — Code quality https://book.the-turing-way.org/reproducible-research/code-quality

Writing clear and maintainable code

Guidelines for writing good R code https://www.r-bloggers.com/2022/02/guidelines-for-writing-good-r-code/

Documenting code as part of code quality

Beginner’s guide to documentation (Write the Docs) https://www.writethedocs.org/guide/writing/beginners-guide-to-docs/

How to use these resources



Focus first on readability, structure, basic testing, and documentation. Advanced optimisation or tooling should be driven by demonstrated need, not habit.

text:
Responsible and inclusive research
Supports Chapter 8
Responsible research involves clear roles, accountability, and escalation routes, alongside ethical and inclusive practice. The resources below support decision-making, governance, and responsible restriction.

title: Resources and Further Reading
text:
Automation and workflows
Supports Chapter 9
Automation supports reproducibility by making dependencies, execution order, and outputs explicit. These resources provide practical entry points at different levels of complexity.

Workflow tools (use when complexity increases)

targets (R) https://docs.ropensci.org/targets/
Snakemake https://snakemake.readthedocs.io/

renv (R) https://rstudio.github.io/renv/

Further reading

Workflow concepts overview https://book.the-turing-way.org/reproducible-research/ci/ci-github-actions/#id-1-workflow https://pure.manchester.ac.uk/ws/portalfiles/portal/783113834/2410.03490v1.pdf https://tm.web.ox.ac.uk/blog/workflows-how-do-researchers-do-their-research-how-does-collaboration-work
Continuous integration for research (Advanced / reference) https://book.the-turing-way.org/reproducible-research/ci







Proportionate automation



If automation makes your work harder to understand, it is too heavy. Start with scripts, add structure when needed, and document decisions as you go.

title: Resources and Further Reading
section: Publishing, PIDs, repositories, licensing, and reuse
text:
Publishing, PIDs, repositories, licensing, and reuse
Supports Chapter 10

Persistent identifiers and citation

ORCID — researcher identifiers https://orcid.org
DataCite — DOIs and dataset metadata https://datacite.org
ROR — organisational identifiers https://ror.org
Zenodo ↔︎ GitHub: minting DOIs for code releases https://docs.github.com/en/repositories/archiving-a-github-repository/referencing-and-citing-content
Citable research objects (The Turing Way) https://book.the-turing-way.org/communication/citable
Data and software citation guidance (The Turing Way) https://book.the-turing-way.org/communication/citable/citable-steps



Preprints and early dissemination

Preprints overview (The Turing Way) https://book.the-turing-way.org/reproducible-research/open/open-access/#rr-open-access-preprints



Reporting guidelines for health and population research
This section provides orientation and signposting. Detailed application and worked examples belong in Chapter 10.
High-quality research is only useful if it is reported transparently. Reporting guidelines specify the minimum information needed to describe how a study was designed, conducted, analysed, and interpreted.
More than 250 reporting guidelines exist, curated by the EQUATOR Network (Enhancing the QUAlity and Transparency Of health Research).
EQUATOR Network — reporting guidelines library10
Core guideline families (commonly used):

CONSORT — randomised controlled trials
SPIRIT — protocols for interventional trials
STROBE — observational studies (cohort, case–control, cross-sectional)
PRISMA — systematic reviews and meta-analyses (incl. extensions such as PRISMA-S)
MOOSE — meta-analyses of observational studies
STARD — diagnostic accuracy studies

Overview resource (optional):11


Repositories and access routes

UCL Research Data Repository https://www.ucl.ac.uk/library/open-science-research-support/research-data-management/ucl-research-data-repository
Choosing a repository (The Turing Way) https://book.the-turing-way.org/reproducible-research/rdm/rdm-repository/



Licensing and reuse (core)

Copyright and your research publications (UCL) https://www.ucl.ac.uk/library/learning-teaching-support/ucl-copyright-advice/copyright-and-your-research-publications
Licensing research outputs (The Turing Way) https://book.the-turing-way.org/reproducible-research/licensing
Creative Commons licences (overview) https://creativecommons.org/licenses/
Open-source software licences (chooser) https://choosealicense.com/
Licence compatibility and common pitfalls https://book.the-turing-way.org/reproducible-research/licensing/licensing-compatibility



Copyright training

UCL Copyright training https://www.ucl.ac.uk/library/learning-teaching-support/ucl-copyright-advice/copyright-training
UCL Copyright Essentials 2023–2024 https://lccos.ucl.ac.uk/library/articulate/copyright-essentials/#/







Practical licensing rule



Pick a licence per output type (text, data/metadata, code) and record it alongside access conditions. If you cannot licence something, record why and what can be shared instead (e.g., metadata + code + synthetic example).

title: Resources and Further Reading
text:
Repository “minimum viable release” guidance
When you release code/data/materials to a repository, include at least:

README (purpose, how to run/use, inputs/outputs)
CITATION information (e.g., CITATION.cff)
Licence file
Version tag/release notes (what changed)
Environment notes (how to recreate the software environment)
Data documentation and access statement (even if data are restricted)

title: Resources and Further Reading
section: Common pitfalls (and how to avoid them)
text:
Common pitfalls (and how to avoid them)

No clear source of truth → agree repository/shared drive rules early.
Raw data overwritten → lock raw data; derive via scripts.
Unstated restrictions → document constraints + access route + rationale.
No licence → users assume reuse is not allowed.
No environment notes → code becomes “works on my machine”.
Outputs not linked → use DOIs/ORCID; reference related objects in metadata/README.








Keep this page current



Link to this page from your project README.md or internal wiki. When policies or tools change, update here once — every Chapter and project benefits.

Data Management Plans

How this links to the rest of the course




Chapter 4 focuses on what the DMP must commit to and how assessors interpret it.
Chapter 5 owns minimum essential metadata: see Essential metadata.
Chapter 6 covers documentation artefacts (READMEs, ELNs, SOPs): see Documentation, ELNs, and SOPs.






Derived and intermediate data: retention decision prompts (quick guide)
Use these prompts when writing the DMP sections on retention, preservation, and storage.
Record explicitly:

which derived datasets are considered analysis-critical (needed to reproduce results);
which intermediate files are recomputable (can be regenerated from scripts + inputs);
what will be retained during the project vs after project end;
expected access model (open / safeguarded / controlled / mediated) for each retained component;
how provenance will be captured at a high level (e.g., “derived datasets are generated by scripted workflows and versioned”).

Chapter 5 covers how to implement raw/derived organisation and provenance in practice; the DMP should state the commitment, scope, and rationale.
